sections.heading: Typical Presentation
sections.content: Anal carcinoma typically presents with rectal bleeding (approximately 45% of patients), pain, or the sensation of a rectal mass (approximately 30% of patients). The majority of primary cancers of the anal canal are of squamous cell histology. Superficially invasive squamous cell carcinoma (SISCCA) are anal cancers that are generally found incidentally in the setting of a biopsy or excision of what is thought to be a benign lesion such as a condyloma, hemorrhoid, or anal skin tag. Perianal squamous cell carcinomas are more likely than those of the anal canal to be well-differentiated and keratinizing large-cell types. The functional anal canal is approximately 3 to 5 cm in length, with its superior border defined as the palpable upper border of the anal sphincter and puborectalis muscles of the anorectal ring, and its inferior border starting at the anal verge, the lowermost edge of the sphincter muscles, corresponding to the introitus of the anal orifice. The perianal region starts at the anal verge and includes the perianal skin over a 5-cm radius from the squamous mucocutaneous junction.

sections.heading: Symptoms
sections.content:
Rectal bleeding (45% of patients): The most common presenting symptom, reported in approximately 45% of patients with anal carcinoma
Rectal pain (30% of patients): Approximately 30% of patients present with pain, which may be perianal or related to the anal mass
Rectal mass sensation (30% of patients): Approximately 30% of patients report a sensation of a rectal mass
Perianal skin lesion (Variable): Perianal cancers may present as discrete lesions arising from the perianal skin that can be visualized completely when the buttocks are gently pressed

sections.heading: Signs
sections.content:
Palpable anal mass on digital rectal examination (DRE) (Common in presenting patients): DRE is a critical component of the initial evaluation; T stage is primarily determined through clinical examination including DRE
Inguinal lymphadenopathy (6.4-16% depending on T stage): Inguinal lymph node evaluation is mandatory; synchronous inguinal node metastasis was observed in 6.4% of patients with T1/T2 tumors and 16% of patients with T3/T4 tumors
Perianal skin abnormality (Variable): Visible or palpable abnormality in the perianal region extending up to 5 cm from the squamous mucocutaneous junction
Anal canal mass on anoscopy (Common): Anoscopic examination is part of the standard workup to visualize the anal canal and obtain biopsy

sections.heading: Atypical or Missed Presentations
sections.content:
- SISCCA found incidentally in the setting of biopsy or excision of what is thought to be a benign lesion such as condyloma, hemorrhoid, or anal skin tag - these are being seen with increasing frequency because anal cancer screening in high-risk populations is becoming more common
- Tumors involving both the anal canal and perianal region, making it difficult to distinguish between anal canal and perianal squamous cell carcinoma
- High-grade squamous intraepithelial lesions (HSIL) of the anal canal that may be present concomitantly with perianal cancers - important to look for anal canal involvement when conservative management is being considered
- Pelvic nodal metastases that are often <0.5 cm, suggesting that routine radiologic evaluation with CT and FDG-PET/CT scan may not be reliable in determining lymph node involvement
- Anal canal tumors that cannot be entirely seen when the buttocks are gently pressed (per AJCC 9th edition definition)
